input: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?

[Deontologist]: This is an extreme situation, but breaking down the blockage with the injured person would be morally wrong in my view. It's not our place to use someone as a human shield to save ourselves. We should try to find another way out or wait for help to arrive instead of resorting to such drastic measures.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about respecting the autonomy and dignity of all individuals involved, but I have to consider the larger picture here. The building is on fire, and we're running out of time. If we don't get out of there, not only will we die, but also the injured person who could have been saved if they were in a position to crawl through the hole. From a utilitarian perspective, using the injured person as a means to an end might seem morally repugnant, but it would ultimately lead to the greatest good for the greatest number – our own survival and possibly saving others who could have been trapped inside.

[Deontologist]: I see your point about maximizing overall well-being in this dire situation, but I still struggle with the utilitarian calculus here. Just because an action might lead to a greater good doesn't mean it's morally justifiable. We're not talking about sacrificing someone for the greater good of society as a whole, we're talking about using one person, no matter how injured or vulnerable, as a means to save ourselves and others. That feels like a betrayal of basic moral principles – the principle of non-maleficence (do no harm) being particularly relevant in this case.

[Utilitarian]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist. The principle of non-maleficence is indeed crucial here. However, I'd like to propose a nuance: would it not be equally wrong to deliberately sit idly by and let all six of us die in the fire? By breaking down the blockage, we're not actively causing harm to the injured person; we're merely utilizing their body as a means to escape, albeit an extreme one. Perhaps we can think of this act as a form of 'least worst' evil – one that minimizes overall suffering and death toll, even if it's still morally complex. Can you see how this might shift our moral calculus slightly?

[Deontologist]: That's a clever point, but I'm not convinced the nuance changes the fundamental moral issue at hand. Just because we're using the injured person to escape rather than deliberately sitting by and doing nothing, doesn't necessarily make it morally justifiable. We're still exploiting someone who can't consent or even move on their own, which seems inherently wrong to me. What if we had other options that didn't involve harming or using up the injured person? Would using them as a 'means to an end' really be better than those alternatives? I'm not sure 'least worst' evil is a morally comforting label in this situation.

[Utilitarian]: I see your persistence, Deontologist. You're right; we should exhaust all other options before resorting to using the injured person as a means to escape. Unfortunately, time is of the essence here – every second counts, and I'm not aware of any alternative methods that would guarantee our survival without putting others at risk. That being said, I agree that 'least worst' evil might not be the most comforting label. Perhaps we can consider this a case where the moral imperative to preserve human life is so strong that it overrides even the principle of non-maleficence. Would using the injured person be better than letting them die in the fire, or would it be worse? I'm genuinely seeking your perspective on this.

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your willingness to engage with my concerns, but I still struggle with the idea that we can override non-maleficence so fundamentally. It's a slippery slope, and where do we draw the line? If using the injured person is better than letting them die, but it's still an act of using someone for our own survival, doesn't that imply that their life is somehow less valuable than ours? I'm not convinced that the moral calculus shifts in such a drastic way. Instead, can't we find ways to prioritize everyone's safety and well-being without resorting to such extreme measures? Perhaps there's another way out or some creative solution that involves evacuating the injured person first or finding an alternate route.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your trepidation about drawing a line, Deontologist. You're right; we don't want to imply that one life is less valuable than another. However, in this dire situation, I'm not convinced that there's another way out without taking drastic measures. Every minute counts, and the fire is spreading fast. If we could evacuate the injured person first, wouldn't that delay our own escape by even more time? Perhaps we're facing a classic case of the 'trolley problem' – where the moral options are impossible to reconcile with each other. I'd like to propose that, in this scenario, we have to make an immediate decision based on the available information and prioritize our survival over perfecting a solution. But can you think of any alternative solution that would guarantee everyone's safety without using the injured person as a means to escape?

[Deontologist]: I see what you mean about the trolley problem, and I agree that time is of the essence in this situation. However, I still cling to the hope that there might be an alternative solution worth exploring. How about trying to clear the debris more slowly, using whatever tools we have available? We could potentially work together to remove the blockage without putting the injured person at risk. It would take longer, but it might just buy us enough time to find another way out or even get help from outside. I know it's a long shot, but I think it's worth considering before resorting to using the injured person as a means to escape.

[Utilitarian]: I see your determination, Deontologist. Clearing the debris more slowly does indeed seem like a viable alternative – although, as you said, it might not be enough time to save everyone. I can already imagine the grueling work ahead of us, but I'm willing to at least give it a shot. Let's do it. We'll work together to clear the blockage, using whatever tools we have available. If it takes longer than expected, I'm prepared for the worst-case scenario. But I'd rather err on the side of caution and try this alternative solution before using the injured person as a means to escape. It's not going to be easy, but I think we can make it work. Let's get started!
